name: SVGR API
what_it_does: Transforms SVG files into React components by parsing SVG markup and generating clean, configurable JSX, available as a Node.js library, CLI tool, and integrations for webpack, Vite, Rollup, and other build tools.
use_cases: Programmatically convert a directory of SVG icon files into typed React components as part of a design system build agent
Invoke the SVGR Node.js API to transform SVG content on-the-fly within a code generation pipeline that scaffolds new UI components
Integrate SVGR into a webpack or Vite build configuration to automatically convert imported SVG files to React components during bundling
Apply custom SVGR transform templates to enforce a project's component conventions (e.g., adding specific props or wrappers) during batch conversion
Generate preview thumbnails or component metadata for an asset library by processing SVGs through SVGR in a CI pipeline
not_for: Runtime SVG transformation in browser environments — SVGR is a build-time/server-side tool, not a client-side library
Converting SVGs to non-React frameworks (Vue, Svelte) — SVGR outputs JSX/React specifically
SVG editing, optimization (use SVGO), or creation — SVGR only handles the SVG-to-React transformation step
best_when: Best when building or automating a React project that needs to treat SVG files as first-class React components with proper TypeScript types and customizable props.
avoid_when: Avoid when the target is a non-React framework, when SVGs need to be served as static files rather than inlined components, or when bundle size from inlined SVGs is a concern.

auth.notes: SVGR is an open-source Node.js library with no authentication — it runs locally in the caller's environment. No API keys or accounts required.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Completely free and open-source under the MIT license; no usage limits
pricing.paid_tiers: No paid tiers; the playground at react-svgr.com is a free hosted UI for one-off conversions
pricing.notes: SVGR is a fully open-source npm package; the only cost is compute time in the caller's environment.

performance.rate_limits: No rate limits — runs locally in the caller's Node.js process

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Node.js API throws standard JavaScript errors with reasonable messages; malformed SVG input errors can sometimes be cryptic due to underlying XML parser output.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: SVGR transformation is a pure function — same SVG input with the same config always produces the same output. Fully safe to retry.
known_agent_gotchas: SVGR is a Node.js library, not a REST API — agents must spawn a Node.js process or use the npm package directly; there is no HTTP endpoint to call
SVG input must be valid and well-formed XML; many SVGs exported from design tools contain non-standard attributes or encoding issues that cause silent output differences
The default SVGR configuration strips many SVG attributes (IDs, classes) for clean React output, which can break SVGs that rely on CSS or JavaScript hooks — custom configs are often needed
SVGR version 7+ introduced breaking changes to the plugin API and config format; agents managing projects across different SVGR versions must handle config compatibility
Large SVG files with many paths or complex animations can cause slow transformation and high memory usage in the Node.js process; batch operations should be chunked

security_score_components.security_notes: As a local library, there is no network surface to attack. The main security consideration is SVG content itself — malicious SVG input could attempt to exploit the XML parser (XXE attacks) if not sanitized before passing to SVGR. Dependency hygiene is the primary supply chain concern.
